text: As described in our previous reports, the transfer of health research data from the EU to the US continues to remain a problem. The restrictive GDPR rules combine with ongoing surveillance concerns around the world, in particular in relation to the vast amount of law enforcement and national security data collection authorities have in the US, over European citizens’ personal data. Since the entry into force of the GDPR, many cases have documented how partnerships between the EU and the US have fallen apart or become heavily impaired, especially with US universities and national research laboratories. Although the EU-US adequacy decision, which governed the international transfer of data between EU and US for decades, was replaced by a new one in July 2023, any stability ensured by this new decision will be short-lived, as many lawyers already predict it will be annulled, in the same way as the previous decision, by the EU Court of Justice. Meanwhile, the EHDS regulation which is under discussion at EU level might perhaps be a way to put an end to once and for all to these data transfer controversies between public entities. This policy brief examines the limitations of current EU law in relation to the transfer of personal health data between public entities for health research purposes and draws a roadmap for overcoming the obstacles to data sharing that presently observed in practice.
headline: Fully Human Written
